lessons.title: Script Structure, set -euo pipefail & IFS
lessons.content:
Every robust bash script opens the same way, and as an ex-technician you can treat this as the pre-flight checklist that separates professional glue from a fragile hack.

Start with a portable shebang: `#!/usr/bin/env bash` finds bash via `PATH` instead of hardcoding `/bin/bash`, which matters because bash lives in different places across distros and macOS.

Next, the safety triad `set -euo pipefail`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
IFS=$'\n\t'
```

- `-e` (errexit): abort the moment any command returns non-zero, instead of blindly charging ahead on a failed `cd`.
- `-u` (nounset): treat use of an *unset* variable as an error, catching typos like `$flie` before they silently expand to empty.
- `-o pipefail`: make a pipeline fail if *any* stage fails, not just the last. Without it, `false | true` reports success.

Setting `IFS=$'\n\t'` (newline and tab only, dropping the default space) is the classic defense against word-splitting filenames that contain spaces.

These are not magic — `set -e` has well-known blind spots (it won't trigger inside `if`/`&&` tests, and a function's failure can be masked), which is exactly why you still write explicit checks and run ShellCheck. Think of `set -euo pipefail` as turning your script from "keep going and hope" into "fail fast and loud," the same discipline as a built-in test that halts on the first out-of-spec reading rather than shipping the unit.

items.front: Why use `#!/usr/bin/env bash` instead of `#!/bin/bash`?
items.back: `env` resolves `bash` through `PATH`, so the script runs against whichever bash is installed rather than assuming a fixed path. bash lives in different locations across distros and macOS (often a newer one under `/usr/local` or Homebrew), so the env form is more portable.

items.front: Expand the three options in `set -euo pipefail`.
items.back: `-e` (errexit): exit on any command failing. `-u` (nounset): error on referencing an unset variable. `-o pipefail`: a pipeline returns the first non-zero exit of any stage, not just the last command's status.

items.front: What problem does setting `IFS=$'\n\t'` solve?
items.back: The default `IFS` includes the space, so unquoted expansions get word-split on spaces — breaking iteration over filenames like `my file.txt`. Restricting IFS to newline and tab stops space-splitting. (Still prefer quoting and `while read -r` / null-delimited iteration.)

items.front: Give one well-known blind spot of `set -e`.
items.back: `set -e` does NOT trigger when a command runs as the condition of `if`, `while`, `until`, or on the left of `&&`/`||`. A failing command inside those contexts is treated as a normal boolean test, so errors there are silently tolerated.

items.prompt: Without `pipefail`, what does `false | true` return as the pipeline's exit status?
choices: 0, the status of the last command (true)
items.explanation: By default a pipeline's exit status is the status of its LAST command, so `false | true` reports 0 (success) and the upstream failure is hidden. `set -o pipefail` changes this so the pipeline reflects the first failing stage.
`set -u` (nounset) makes referencing an unset variable a fatal error ("unbound variable"), catching typos. Use `${undefined_var:-}` to intentionally allow an empty default under nounset.

items.prompt: Which line belongs at the very TOP of a robust bash script?
choices: set +e to keep going on errors
items.explanation: The portable shebang `#!/usr/bin/env bash` must be the first line, immediately followed by `set -euo pipefail` to enable fail-fast behavior before any real work runs. `trap` comes after, once cleanup targets exist.

items.front: If `set -euo pipefail` is so good, why still run ShellCheck and write explicit tests?
items.back: `set -e` has documented blind spots (conditions, command substitutions, functions called in tests) and can lull you into a false sense of safety. ShellCheck statically catches quoting, word-splitting, and logic bugs that runtime flags miss, so the two are complementary, not redundant.

items.front: What is the difference between `set -e` and `set -x`?
items.back: `set -e` controls error behavior (exit on failure). `set -x` is a *debugging* trace: it prints each command (with expansions) to stderr before running it. They are orthogonal — you often add `set -x` temporarily or run `bash -x script.sh`.

lessons.title: Parameter Expansion & Arrays
lessons.content:
Parameter expansion is bash's built-in string toolkit — using it avoids spawning `sed`/`cut`/`basename` subprocesses and keeps scripts fast and quote-safe.

Defaults and assertions:

```bash
name=${1:-world}        # use $1, or 'world' if unset/empty
: "${API_KEY:?must set API_KEY}"  # abort with message if unset
name=${name:=guest}     # assign default into the variable
```

Trimming with patterns (greedy doubles the symbol):

```bash
path=/var/log/app.log
${path##*/}   # app.log   (strip longest leading */)
${path%/*}    # /var/log  (strip shortest trailing /*)
${path%.log}  # /var/log/app (strip suffix)
```

Substitution and slicing:

```bash
${path/log/LOG}   # replace first match
${path//\//_}     # replace all '/' with '_'
${path:5:3}       # substring: offset 5, length 3
${#path}          # string length
```

Arrays are essential for building argument lists safely — never store multiple arguments in a single space-separated string.

```bash
files=(report.txt "my notes.md" data.csv)
echo "${files[@]}"     # each element quoted separately
echo "${#files[@]}"    # element count
files+=(extra.log)     # append
```

Associative arrays (bash 4+, declare them):

```bash
declare -A age
age[alice]=30; age[bob]=25
for k in "${!age[@]}"; do echo "$k=${age[$k]}"; done
```

The golden rule: always expand with `"${arr[@]}"` (quoted, `@`, not `*`) so each element survives spaces intact — the array equivalent of the quoting discipline that keeps filenames safe.

items.front: What does `${var:-default}` do, and how does `${var:=default}` differ?
items.back: `${var:-default}` expands to `default` if `var` is unset or empty but leaves `var` unchanged. `${var:=default}` does the same expansion AND assigns `default` into `var` as a side effect.

items.front: For `f=/var/log/app.log`, what do `${f##*/}` and `${f%/*}` produce?
items.back: `${f##*/}` strips the longest leading match of `*/`, giving `app.log` (basename). `${f%/*}` strips the shortest trailing match of `/*`, giving `/var/log` (dirname). `#` trims from the front, `%` from the back; doubling makes it greedy.

items.front: Difference between `${path/old/new}` and `${path//old/new}`?
items.back: `${path/old/new}` replaces only the FIRST occurrence of `old`; `${path//old/new}` replaces ALL occurrences. Anchors: `${path/#old/new}` matches at the start, `${path/%old/new}` at the end.

items.front: How do you abort a script with a custom message if `API_KEY` is unset?
items.back: Use the error expansion: `: "${API_KEY:?must set API_KEY}"`. If `API_KEY` is unset or empty, bash prints the message to stderr and exits non-zero. The leading `:` is a no-op command so the expansion is the only effect.

items.prompt: Why expand an array as `"${arr[@]}"` rather than `${arr[*]}` or unquoted?
choices: `"${arr[@]}"` yields each element as a separate, intact word even with spaces
They are identical in all cases
items.explanation: Quoted `"${arr[@]}"` produces one shell word per element, preserving spaces inside elements — exactly what you want when passing an argument list. `"${arr[*]}"` joins all elements into one string (separated by the first IFS char), and unquoted forms word-split, breaking elements with spaces.

items.prompt: You need a key/value map keyed by username. What declaration do you use?
items.explanation: `declare -A` creates an associative array (string-keyed map), available in bash 4+. `declare -a` (lowercase) makes an indexed array. Associative arrays MUST be declared with `-A` before use; you cannot create one implicitly.
Substring expansion `${s:offset:length}` starts at offset 1 (the second char, 'e') and takes 3 characters: 'e', 'l', 'l' -> `ell`. `${#s}` would give the length, 5.

items.front: How do you iterate over the KEYS of an associative array `age`?
items.back: Use the `!` (indirection) form: `for k in "${!age[@]}"; do echo "$k -> ${age[$k]}"; done`. `${!age[@]}` lists keys; `${age[@]}` lists values. The quoting keeps keys with spaces intact.

items.front: Why prefer parameter expansion over piping to `sed`/`basename` for simple string work?
items.back: Parameter expansion is built into the shell, so it avoids forking a subprocess per operation (faster in loops) and sidesteps an extra layer of quoting/escaping. For trimming paths, defaults, and simple replacement it is cleaner and safer than external tools.

lessons.title: Control Flow, Functions, getopts & Safe File Loops
lessons.content:
With safety and strings handled, structure the logic. Use `[[ ]]` for tests, not the old `[ ]`/`test`:

```bash
if [[ -f $file && $count -gt 0 ]]; then ...; fi
```

`[[ ]]` is a bash keyword: it does no word-splitting or glob expansion on its operands (so unquoted `$file` is safe), supports `&&`/`||`/`==` pattern matching and `=~` regex. Plain `[ ]` is just the `test` command — it word-splits, needs every variable quoted, and is only worth it for strict POSIX sh. For integer math use the arithmetic context `(( ))`:

```bash
if (( count > 0 )); then ((total += count)); fi
```

`case` beats long if-chains for dispatch, and functions keep things tidy — always declare loop and temp variables `local`:

```bash
log() { printf '%s\n' "$*" >&2; }   # log to stderr
backup() {
  local src=$1 dest=$2
  cp -a -- "$src" "$dest"
}
```

Functions return an *exit code* (0-255) via `return`; emit real data on stdout and capture with `$(func)`.

Parse flags with `getopts` (POSIX, short options):

```bash
while getopts ":o:v" opt; do
  case $opt in
    o) out=$OPTARG ;;
    v) verbose=1 ;;
    *) echo "usage: ..." >&2; exit 2 ;;
  esac
done
shift $((OPTIND - 1))
```

The single most important loop idiom is safe filename iteration — never `for f in $(ls)`. Use `while read -r` with a null delimiter:

```bash
while IFS= read -r -d '' f; do
  printf 'processing %s\n' "$f"
done < <(find . -type f -print0)
```

`-r` keeps backslashes literal, `IFS=` preserves leading/trailing whitespace, `-d ''` splits on NUL so spaces and newlines in names are harmless.

items.front: Name two concrete advantages of `[[ ]]` over `[ ]` in bash.
items.back: 1) `[[ ]]` does no word-splitting or glob expansion on its operands, so unquoted `$var` is safe and empty values don't break the test. 2) It supports `&&`, `||`, `==` glob pattern matching and `=~` regex directly. `[ ]` is the external/builtin `test` and requires careful quoting.

items.front: When would you still use `[ ]` instead of `[[ ]]`?
items.back: When the script must be portable POSIX `sh` (e.g. `#!/bin/sh`, running under dash/busybox), since `[[ ]]` is a bash/ksh extension not in POSIX. In that case use `[ ]` and quote every variable.

items.front: Why declare variables `local` inside functions?
items.back: Bash variables are global by default, so an unmarked assignment inside a function can clobber a caller's variable (especially loop counters like `i`). `local` scopes the variable to the function, preventing action-at-a-distance bugs.

items.front: How does a bash function return data vs. a status?
items.back: `return N` sets the exit STATUS (0-255), used for success/failure, read via `$?`. To return DATA, print it to stdout and capture with command substitution: `result=$(myfunc)`. Conflating the two (e.g. `return $bignum`) truncates mod 256.
items.hint: stdout for data, return for status.

items.prompt: Why is `for f in $(ls)` an unsafe way to loop over files?
choices: The unquoted command substitution word-splits on IFS and glob-expands, breaking on filenames with spaces, newlines, or shell metacharacters
items.explanation: `$(ls)` output is word-split on IFS (spaces, tabs, newlines) and then pathname-expanded, so any filename containing whitespace or glob chars is mangled or split into multiple iterations. Use `while IFS= read -r -d '' f; do ...; done < <(find . -print0)` or a plain glob `for f in *`.

items.prompt: In the safe loop `while IFS= read -r -d '' f`, what does the `-r` flag do?
choices: Prevents backslashes from being interpreted as escape characters
items.explanation: `-r` (raw) tells `read` not to treat backslashes as escapes, so a filename like `a\b` is read literally. `IFS=` preserves leading/trailing whitespace, and `-d ''` sets a NUL delimiter to match `find -print0`. You should essentially always use `read -r`.

items.prompt: After a `getopts` parsing loop, why run `shift $((OPTIND - 1))`?
choices: To discard the parsed options so the remaining positional arguments start at $1
items.explanation: `getopts` advances `OPTIND` past the options it consumed. `shift $((OPTIND - 1))` drops those, so `$1`, `$2`, ... and `$@` now refer to the non-option (positional) arguments — e.g. the file paths after the flags.

items.prompt: Which construct is the right tool for integer comparison in bash?
items.explanation: The arithmetic context `(( a > b ))` does numeric comparison and lets you write `count`, `total += 1`, etc. naturally. Inside `[[ ]]`, `>` is a STRING (lexical) comparison, so `[[ 9 > 10 ]]` is true lexically — a classic bug. Use `(( ))` for math, `[[ ]]` for strings/files.

items.front: Why send log/diagnostic output to stderr (`>&2`) in a function meant to also return data?
items.back: If the function's data is captured via `$(func)`, anything printed to stdout becomes part of the captured value. Routing logs to stderr (`printf ... >&2`) keeps them visible on the terminal / journald without polluting the captured result.

lessons.title: Traps, ShellCheck, Process Substitution & the Python Boundary
lessons.content:
Production scripts clean up after themselves. `trap` registers a handler that runs on signals or pseudo-signals:

```bash
tmp=$(mktemp -d)
cleanup() { rm -rf -- "$tmp"; }
trap cleanup EXIT          # runs on normal exit, error, or Ctrl-C
trap 'echo "failed at line $LINENO" >&2' ERR
```

`trap ... EXIT` is the workhorse: it fires no matter how the script ends (success, `set -e` abort, or signal), so a temp dir is always removed — the scripting analog of a `finally` block, and the same discipline as powering down a test rig cleanly.

Process substitution `<(...)` lets a command read another command's output as if it were a file, avoiding temp files and the subshell-variable-loss of pipes:

```bash
diff <(sort a.txt) <(sort b.txt)
while read -r line; do count=$line; done < <(wc -l < big.log)
```

Before shipping, **run ShellCheck** (`shellcheck script.sh`) — it statically flags unquoted expansions, useless `cat`, missing `read -r`, and `set -e` gotchas; treat a clean ShellCheck pass as mandatory, like a clean lint. For runtime issues, `bash -x script.sh` or `set -x` traces execution.

The most important judgment call is **when to abandon bash for Python**. Reach for Python (`subprocess`, `pathlib`, `argparse`, `json`, `venv`) once a script: grows past ~50-100 lines; needs real data structures (lists of dicts, nested JSON); does HTTP/API calls or parses structured data; needs proper error handling, retries, or unit tests; or has quoting that keeps biting you. Bash excels at gluing CLI tools and short pipelines; Python excels at logic, data, and testability. Recognizing that boundary early is itself a senior skill.

items.front: Why is `trap cleanup EXIT` preferred over putting `rm -rf "$tmp"` at the end of the script?
items.back: `EXIT` fires on EVERY exit path — normal completion, a `set -e` abort, an error, or a caught signal — so the temp dir is removed even when the script dies early. A cleanup line at the bottom is skipped on any premature exit. It is bash's `finally`.
items.hint: What happens if the script aborts halfway?

items.front: What does the `ERR` trap give you that `EXIT` does not?
items.back: `trap '...' ERR` runs specifically when a command fails (returns non-zero) under `set -e`, letting you log context like `$LINENO` or the failing command before exiting. `EXIT` runs unconditionally and can't distinguish success from failure on its own (you check `$?`).

items.front: What does process substitution `<(cmd)` provide?
items.back: It runs `cmd` and exposes its output via a filename (a `/dev/fd/...` path or FIFO) so another command can read it as a file — e.g. `diff <(sort a) <(sort b)`. It also avoids the pipe pitfall where `cmd | while read` runs the loop in a subshell and loses variable changes.

items.front: What is ShellCheck and why run it on every script?
items.back: ShellCheck is a static analyzer for shell scripts. It catches unquoted variables, missing `read -r`, useless `cat`, `set -e` blind spots, and dozens of common bugs before runtime. A clean ShellCheck pass is the shell equivalent of a clean lint and should be treated as mandatory.

items.prompt: Which is the STRONGEST signal that you should rewrite a bash script in Python?
choices: It uses more than one pipe
It needs to parse and manipulate nested JSON / structured data with real error handling
items.explanation: Nested data structures, JSON/HTTP, real error handling, and testability are exactly where bash gets painful and Python (json, requests/urllib, dataclasses, try/except, unit tests) shines. Pipes, loops, and calling grep are bread-and-butter bash and not reasons to switch.

items.prompt: Which Python standard-library trio replaces the typical 'shell out + manipulate paths + parse flags' job when porting a script?
items.explanation: `subprocess` (run external commands safely with argument lists, no shell quoting), `pathlib` (object-oriented path/file handling), and `argparse` (declarative flag parsing with help and validation) are the modern idiomatic replacements for shelling out, path munging, and getopts.

items.prompt: How do you trace exactly which commands a script runs, with their expanded arguments?
items.explanation: `bash -x script.sh` (or `set -x` within the script) prints each command to stderr after expansion, just before executing it — the go-to runtime trace. ShellCheck is static (no execution); `set -e` controls error behavior, not tracing.

items.prompt: Why does `count=0; ... | while read x; do ((count++)); done; echo $count` print 0?
choices: The piped `while` runs in a SUBSHELL, so its `count` changes don't reach the parent
items.explanation: In a pipeline, each stage runs in a subshell, so the loop's `count` increments are lost when the subshell exits. Use process substitution `done < <(cmd)` (loop runs in the current shell) so the variable survives — a key reason `<(...)` exists.

items.front: Summarize the bash-vs-Python boundary heuristic in one sentence.
items.back: Stay in bash for short scripts that glue CLI tools and pipelines together; switch to Python once you need data structures, HTTP/JSON, robust error handling/retries, unit tests, or you've passed roughly 50-100 lines and quoting keeps biting.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Decision diagram contrasting when to stay in bash (gluing CLI tools, short pipelines, always set -euo pipefail and ShellCheck) versus when to switch to Python (JSON/data structures, HTTP, error handling, testing, past ~50-100 lines).
